name: Heavens Gate Full
description: Heaven's Gate was an American UFO religious cult based near San Diego, California. It was founded in the early 1970s and led by Marshall Applewhite (1931–1997) and Bonnie Nettles (1927–1985). The group believed that the Earth was about to be recycled and that the only chance to survive was to leave it immediately. They believed that a spaceship following the Comet Hale-Bopp in 1997 would take them to a higher evolutionary level above human. On March 26, 1997, 39 members of the group committed mass suicide by ingesting phenobarbital mixed with apple sauce and vodka, lying down in bunk beds, and covering themselves with a square purple cloth. They believed that their souls would then be transported to the spaceship. The group's beliefs combined elements of Christian millenarianism, New Age philosophy, and ufology. They saw the Bible as allegorical and believed that Jesus was a space alien sent to Earth. They also believed that the human body was merely a container for the soul, which could be transferred to a higher plane of existence. Mainstream scholars and religious leaders generally view Heaven's Gate as a tragic example of cult behavior and the dangers of religious extremism. They point to Applewhite's charismatic leadership and the group's isolation from the outside world as factors that contributed to the mass suicide. Skeptics also note the lack of verifiable evidence for the group's claims about UFOs and a higher plane of existence. The incident raised important questions about the nature of belief, the power of cults, and the role of technology in religion.
subtitle: UFO cult's mass suicide event.

claims.claim: Hale-Bopp comet was followed by a spaceship to transport souls.
claims.evidence: The group's statements and website content.
claims.criticism: Astronomical observations showed no evidence of a spacecraft.

claims.claim: Physical bodies are 'containers' to be shed for ascension.
claims.evidence: Heaven's Gate teachings, emphasizing transcending earthly existence.
claims.criticism: Contradicts scientific understanding of consciousness and the relationship between mind and body.

claims.claim: Earth was about to be 'recycled' and only escape was through transition.
claims.evidence: The group's apocalyptic worldview and belief in impending planetary destruction.
claims.criticism: Lacks any scientific basis and reflects a distorted interpretation of environmental concerns.

keyFigures.name: Marshall Applewhite
keyFigures.role: Founder and leader of Heaven's Gate

sources.author: Robert Balch
sources.work: Waiting for the Ships: Disillusionment and the Revitalization of Faith in Bo and Peep's UFO Cult
sources.title: Robert Balch

sources.author: Heaven's Gate
sources.work: How and When 'Heaven's Gate' May Be Entered
sources.title: Heaven's Gate
